description: Phase 2 — Evaluation Core: three pure-function modules (Field Interpolator, Condition Evaluator, Active Hours Calculator) with full Vitest unit test coverage.

userStories.title: Field Interpolator — dot-notation + template string resolver (pure function + unit tests)
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a pure field-interpolator module so that condition evaluation, active hours calculation, and action param binding can all resolve event-payload paths and execution-context template strings through a single consistent mechanism.
acceptanceCriteria: src/services/field-interpolator.ts exports type ExecutionContext = { event_id: string; execution_id: string; rule_id: string; rule_version: number }
Exports resolveValue(value: unknown, eventCtx: Record<string, unknown>, execCtx: ExecutionContext): unknown

resolveValue: a string like 'payload.phone' (matches /^[a-zA-Z_][\w.]*$/) is treated as a dot-notation path and resolved via lodash-style deep lookup against eventCtx; returns undefined (not throws) when path is absent
resolveValue: a string containing '{{...}}' tokens (e.g. '{{event_id}}-sms', '{{rule_id}}') has each token replaced with the matching key from execCtx; unknown tokens are left as-is
resolveValue: a string that is neither a dot-path nor contains template tokens is returned as a literal
resolveValue: non-string values (number, boolean, null, object, array) are returned as-is without modification
Exports resolveParams(params: Record<string, unknown>, eventCtx: Record<string, unknown>, execCtx: ExecutionContext): Record<string, unknown>
resolveParams recursively resolves all string values within the params object, including values nested inside child objects and arrays, by calling resolveValue on each leaf string

test/unit/field-interpolator.test.ts covers: dot-notation present, dot-notation missing (returns undefined), deeply nested dot-path, single template token, multiple template tokens in one string, unknown template token left as-is, string literal passthrough, number/boolean/null passthrough, recursive object resolution (nested object), recursive array resolution (array of strings), mixed dot-path inside nested params object
Typecheck passes
Tests pass
userStories.notes: Pure function — no DB, no I/O, no side effects. No external dependencies required; implement dot-path lookup manually (split on '.', reduce). Template tokens: match /\{\{(\w+)\}\}/g, replace from execCtx. This module is imported by Condition Evaluator (US-002) and Active Hours Calculator (US-003).

userStories.title: Condition Evaluator — boolean expression tree evaluator (pure function + unit tests)
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a pure condition-evaluator module so that the Rule Matcher can test whether an incoming event payload satisfies a rule's condition tree before creating an execution.
acceptanceCriteria: src/services/condition-evaluator.ts exports the following TypeScript types: LeafCondition = { field: string; op: 'eq'|'neq'|'in'|'not_in'|'gt'|'gte'|'lt'|'lte'|'contains'|'exists'|'not_exists'; value?: unknown }, GroupCondition = { op: 'AND'|'OR'; conditions: Condition[] }, NotCondition = { op: 'NOT'; condition: Condition }, Condition = LeafCondition | GroupCondition | NotCondition
Exports evaluate(condition: Condition | null | undefined, event: Record<string, unknown>, execCtx: ExecutionContext): boolean
evaluate returns true when condition is null or undefined (no condition = always passes)
Field values are resolved from the event object using resolveValue from field-interpolator (dot-notation path lookup)
eq: resolved field value strictly equals operand; neq: strictly not equal
in: operand is an array and includes the resolved field value; not_in: operand is an array and does not include the resolved field value
gt/gte/lt/lte: numeric comparison between resolved field value (coerced to number) and operand (coerced to number)
contains: resolved field value is a string and includes operand string, OR resolved field value is an array and includes operand
exists: resolved field value is not undefined and not null; not_exists: resolved field value is undefined or null
AND: returns true only if all sub-conditions in conditions[] evaluate to true (short-circuits on first false)
OR: returns true if at least one sub-condition in conditions[] evaluates to true (short-circuits on first true)
NOT: returns the negation of the single nested condition
Nested grouping: AND containing OR containing NOT evaluates correctly
Missing field (path resolves to undefined) for comparison operators (eq, gt, in, contains, etc.) returns false without throwing

test/unit/condition-evaluator.test.ts covers: each of the 11 leaf operators (both truthy and falsy case), AND (all-true, one-false), OR (one-true, all-false), NOT (true→false, false→true), nested AND/OR/NOT three levels deep, missing field returns false, null condition returns true, field resolved via dot-path from event payload
Typecheck passes
Tests pass
userStories.notes: Import ExecutionContext and resolveValue from ./field-interpolator.js. Pure function — no DB, no I/O. The execCtx param is threaded through so that field paths that reference execution context keys (e.g. '{{event_id}}') also work inside condition field paths, though this is uncommon.

userStories.title: Active Hours Calculator — delay-until-window-open computation (pure function + unit tests)
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a pure active-hours module so that send_message and send_email workers can determine whether to execute immediately or delay until the next window-open time in the correct timezone.
acceptanceCriteria: src/services/active-hours.ts exports type ActiveHoursConfig = { start: string; end: string; timezone_field: string } where start and end are 'HH:MM' 24-hour strings
Exports computeDelay(config: ActiveHoursConfig, eventPayload: Record<string, unknown>, execCtx: ExecutionContext, now?: Date): number
Returns 0 (execute immediately) when the current time in the resolved timezone is within [start, end) window
Returns milliseconds until the next occurrence of start time when the current time is before the window opens on the same calendar day
Returns milliseconds until the next occurrence of start time on the following calendar day when the current time is after the window has closed (delay always ≤ 86400000 ms / 24 h)
timezone_field is resolved from eventPayload via resolveValue from field-interpolator
When the resolved timezone string is missing, empty, or invalid (not a recognised IANA timezone), logs a console.warn and falls back to UTC
start === end is treated as an always-open window (returns 0 immediately)
Uses the Intl.DateTimeFormat API (available in Node 24) for timezone-aware time-of-day calculation — no additional timezone library required
add luxon as a dependency ONLY if Intl-based DST handling proves insufficient; otherwise use native Intl

test/unit/active-hours.test.ts injects a fixed `now` Date via the optional parameter to make all tests deterministic; covers: current time inside window (returns 0), current time before window on same day (returns correct positive ms), current time after window end (delay rolls to next day, ≤ 86400000 ms), window crossing midnight (e.g. start 22:00, end 06:00 next day), missing/invalid timezone falls back to UTC and returns a number (no throw), start === end returns 0
DST edge case test: use a timezone with a known DST transition (e.g. America/New_York) and a `now` that is at a DST boundary — assert returned delay is > 0 and ≤ 86400000
Typecheck passes
Tests pass
userStories.notes: Import ExecutionContext and resolveValue from ./field-interpolator.js. Pure function — no DB, no I/O. The `now` optional param exists solely for test determinism; workers call computeDelay() without it (defaults to new Date()). The function does NOT schedule anything — it only returns a delay number. Workers own the BullMQ delay call.
